text: The surgeon begins the perineal phase by closing the anus and performing an extra-sphincteric dissection. The skin is opened using a diathermy, and a Lone Star retractor is applied for optimal exposure. A running suture is placed on the skin edge before inserting the gel point port, ensuring a secure seal. Pneumopelvis is initiated with a pressure of twelve. Dissection starts posteriorly at the coccyx level, following the TME plane. The pelvic floor is transected laterally, and muscles on the right side are also transected according to ELAP principles.

The surgeon continues to follow the TME plane cranially, joining the previous dissection. The levator muscles on the left side are completely divided using a hook. The anterior plane dissection is resumed with the hook, while managing the pneumopelvis stability and smoke. Lateral dissection is performed to avoid deep working areas. Anterior dissection proceeds with caution around the prostate to prevent bleeding and nerve injury. Posteriorly, the junction with the abdominal team is achieved, who assist by retracting the specimen. The anterior plane is reopened, and communication with the abdominal team is established. The last lateral attachments are divided using a ligature. The specimen is freed and extracted through the anus with an Alexi wound protector. Hemostasis is checked, a full washout is performed, and a routine drain is placed in the pelvis through a five-millimeter port. An end colostomy is formed, and the perineal wound is closed in layers.
